definition: To move or travel from one place to another; to proceed or leave.
commonUses: Talking about a state or condition changing ('go wrong', 'go bad')
beginnerSentences: I go to school every day.
She goes to the market on Sundays.
We go home at six o'clock.
He went to bed early last night.
They have gone to the airport.
Do you go to the gym often?
I am going to the store.
My father goes to work by bus.
intermediateSentences: I've been going to this doctor for years, and she's always thorough.
The meeting went smoothly, and everyone left satisfied.
If the plan goes well, we'll expand to two more cities.
She's going through a difficult time right now.
The milk has gone bad, so we need to throw it out.
He's going to great lengths to fix this problem.
Things went downhill after the manager left.
I'm not sure this color goes with the curtains.
The negotiations are going nowhere without more information.
We should let the matter go and move forward.
businessSentences: The project is going according to schedule.
Let's go over the quarterly numbers before the call.
I'll go ahead and send the proposal today.
The client wants to go with a different vendor.
We need to go through the contract line by line.
Profits went up by fifteen percent this quarter.
She's going to present the findings at the board meeting.
Let's go with plan B if the client doesn't respond.
The budget approval has gone through finance.
I'll go check with the team and get back to you.
questions: Where do you usually go on weekends?
What time did you go to bed last night?
Have you ever gone abroad for work?
How often do you go to the gym?
Where are you going after this meeting?
Did the presentation go well?
What made you go into this profession?
How did the interview go?
Are you going to the office tomorrow?
Who did you go with to the conference?

commonMistakes.why: 'Go' is an irregular verb - its past tense is 'went', not 'goed'. Irregular verbs don't follow the add '-ed' rule, so they must be memorized individually.

commonMistakes.wrong: She go to school every day.
commonMistakes.right: She goes to school every day.
commonMistakes.why: With he/she/it in the present simple, the verb needs an 's' - 'goes', not 'go'.
After 'have/has', use the past participle (V3) 'gone', not the base form 'go'.

grammarTips: 'Go' is one of the most irregular verbs in English - go, went, gone - so it's worth memorizing these three forms as a set rather than guessing a pattern.
Use 'going to' for planned future actions ('I'm going to call him'), and 'will' for spontaneous decisions or predictions ('I think it will rain').
'Go' pairs with many prepositions to create different meanings: go out (leave for fun), go on (continue), go through (experience or examine), go over (review).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Are you going to the party tonight?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I'm not sure yet. Are you going?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Yeah, I think I'll go for a couple of hours.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Okay, let me know how it goes!

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'Yesterday, I ___ to the market.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Went' is the correct past simple (V2) form of 'go'.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: She go to work by car.
She goes to work by car.
She going to work by car.
She gone to work by car.
miniQuiz.answer: She goes to work by car.
miniQuiz.explanation: With 'she' (third person singular) in the present simple, the verb needs an 's': 'goes'.

miniQuiz.question: I have already ___ (go) to that restaurant twice.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle (V3) form: 'gone'.

tenseUsage.past: I went to the market yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I go to the market every day.
tenseUsage.future: I will go to the market tomorrow.

definition: To exist; to have a particular identity, quality, or state; used as a linking verb to connect a subject with a description.
commonUses: Forming the passive voice (be + past participle)
beginnerSentences: I was happy to see you.
The book is on the table.
intermediateSentences: I've been working here for three years.
She was being unusually quiet during the meeting.
If I were you, I would apologize first.
The report is being reviewed by the legal team.
He's been through a lot this year.
We were about to leave when you called.
This has been one of the busiest weeks of the year.
The decision will be made by Friday.
They've always been supportive of new ideas.
It would be great if you could join us.
businessSentences: The meeting is scheduled for 3 PM.
This proposal has been approved by the board.
We are currently reviewing the budget.
The client was satisfied with the final deliverable.
I am confident this approach will work.
The report is being finalized as we speak.
Our goal is to increase revenue by 20 percent.
The team has been working overtime this month.
This issue is a top priority for us.
The contract will be signed next week.
questions: Where are you from?
How long have you been working here?
What time is the meeting?
Were you at the conference last week?
Is this seat taken?
How was your weekend?
Are you free this afternoon?
Have you ever been to Japan?
Who is responsible for this project?
What would you be doing if not this job?

commonMistakes.why: 'Are' goes with 'you/we/they', but 'she' (third person singular) needs 'is'.
'Agree' is already a full verb - don't add 'am' before it. Only use 'am/is/are' before adjectives, nouns, or -ing forms, not before ordinary verbs.
'Know' is a stative verb (describing a mental state, not an action), so it's not normally used in the continuous (-ing) form.

grammarTips: 'Be' changes form more than any other English verb: am, is, are, was, were, been, being - there's no way around memorizing all of them.
Use 'been' after 'have/has/had' (I have been there), and 'being' after am/is/are/was/were for the continuous or passive (She is being helpful).
Stative verbs like 'know', 'want', 'believe', and 'love' are rarely used with 'be + -ing' because they describe states, not actions in progress.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Hi, how are you?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I'm good, thanks! How about you?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: I'm doing well. Is this your first time here?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Yes, it is. I'm really enjoying it so far.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct form: 'They ___ at the park now.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'They' takes 'are' in the present simple.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Know' is a stative verb and isn't normally used with 'am' or in the -ing form.

miniQuiz.question: This report has ___ (be) reviewed twice already.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'has', use the past participle 'been'.

tenseUsage.past: I was tired yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I am tired today.
tenseUsage.future: I will be tired tomorrow after the trip.

definition: To possess, own, or hold something; also used as an auxiliary verb to form perfect tenses.
beginnerSentences: He had a good time at the party.
intermediateSentences: I've had this phone for almost three years now.
She has to finish the report before she leaves.
We've had some disagreements, but we always work it out.
Having reviewed the data, I think we should proceed.
He had already left by the time I arrived.
You'll have plenty of time to prepare before the exam.
I've never had a problem with this supplier before.
They had been waiting for over an hour.
Do you have any experience with this software?
She has a habit of double-checking everything.
businessSentences: We have a call scheduled with the client at noon.
The team has completed the first phase of the project.
I'll have the report ready by tomorrow morning.
We had a productive discussion about the roadmap.
Do you have any concerns about the timeline?
The company has grown significantly this year.
We're having some issues with the server today.
I have a few questions about the proposal.
They've had positive feedback from most clients.
We need to have this finalized by end of week.
questions: Do you have any siblings?
How long have you had this job?
Do you have time to talk right now?
Have you had breakfast yet?
What do you have planned for the weekend?
Does she have any experience in marketing?
Have you ever had to deal with a difficult client?
What do you have to do before the deadline?
Did you have a good trip?
Do we have everything we need for the meeting?

commonMistakes.why: Age in English uses 'be', not 'have' - this is a very common translation mistake from languages where age uses 'have'. Say 'I am' + age, not 'I have' + age.
With he/she/it, 'have' becomes 'has' in the present simple.
Don't mix 'have' with a simple past time word like 'yesterday'. Use plain past tense ('went') for a finished, specific past time; save 'have + past participle' for unspecified or recent past.

grammarTips: 'Have' does double duty in English: as a main verb meaning 'possess' (I have a car), and as a helper verb for perfect tenses (I have finished).
'Have to' expresses obligation, similar to 'must': 'I have to submit this by Friday' means it's required, not optional.
Don't use 'have' to talk about age, hunger, or temperature the way some languages do - English uses 'be' instead: 'I am hungry', not 'I have hunger'.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Do you have a minute to talk?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Sure, I have about ten minutes before my next call.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Great, I just have a couple of quick questions.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: No problem, go ahead.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: English uses 'be' (am/is/are), not 'have', to talk about age.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct form: 'He ___ a meeting every Monday.'
miniQuiz.explanation: With 'he' (third person singular), 'have' becomes 'has'.

miniQuiz.question: By the time we arrived, the meeting had already ___ (start).
miniQuiz.explanation: Past perfect (had + past participle) describes an action completed before another past action.

tenseUsage.past: I had a meeting yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I have a meeting today.
tenseUsage.future: I will have a meeting tomorrow.

definition: To perform an action or task; also used as an auxiliary verb to form questions and negatives in the simple tenses.
commonUses: Forming questions in the simple present/past ('Do you...?', 'Did she...?')
beginnerSentences: I do my homework every evening.
She does the dishes after dinner.
We did our best in the exam.
I am doing my project now.
intermediateSentences: I've done everything I can to help with this project.
She's doing really well in her new role.
Did you manage to finish the report on time?
We're doing a full review of the process next month.
He does his best work early in the morning.
I don't think this approach will work.
What has she done to improve her performance?
They did mention some concerns during the call.
I do believe this is the right decision.
We've been doing this for over five years now.
businessSentences: What does this department do exactly?
We did some research before launching the product.
I'll do a quick review before sending it out.
The team is doing well against this quarter's targets.
Did the client sign off on the final version?
We don't have the budget for that right now.
She's doing a fantastic job leading this project.
What can we do to improve customer satisfaction?
I've done a preliminary analysis of the numbers.
Let's do a quick recap before we wrap up.
questions: What do you do for a living?
Did you finish the assignment?
What are you doing this weekend?
Does this train go to the city center?
How did the presentation go?
What can I do to help?
Have you done this kind of work before?
Do you usually work from home?
What did you do during your last vacation?
Does she know about the change of plans?

commonMistakes.why: With he/she/it, 'don't' becomes 'doesn't'. Using 'don't' with a third-person singular subject is one of the most common ESL errors.
In questions, 'do' comes right after the question word and before the subject: 'What do you do?' not 'What you do do?'
Don't add 'am doing' before an ordinary verb like 'agree'. 'Do' is only needed as a helper for questions/negatives in simple tenses, not as a filler before every verb.

grammarTips: 'Do' as a helper verb disappears in statements but reappears in questions and negatives: 'I like tea' becomes 'Do you like tea?' and 'I don't like tea'.
Never use 'do/does/did' together with another helper like 'am/is/are' or 'have' in the same clause - 'do' has one specific job per sentence.
'Do' can add emphasis to a statement: compare 'I like it' (neutral) with 'I do like it' (strong emphasis, often used to contradict doubt).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: What do you do on weekends?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I usually do some reading or go for a walk. What about you?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: I did some gardening last weekend, actually.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: That sounds relaxing!

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: With 'she' (third person singular), the negative helper is 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct question: '___ you speak French?'
miniQuiz.explanation: With 'you', the question helper in the present simple is 'do'.

miniQuiz.question: I have already ___ (do) my part of the project.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'done'.

tenseUsage.past: I did my homework yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I do my homework every day.
tenseUsage.future: I will do my homework tomorrow.

definition: To obtain, receive, or acquire something; also used to describe a change of state (get tired) or arrival (get home).
commonUses: Talking about obtaining or receiving something
Describing a change of condition ('get sick', 'get better')
Common in informal spoken English as a substitute for 'become', 'buy', 'receive', or 'understand'
beginnerSentences: I get up at seven every morning.
She got a new phone yesterday.
We get home late on Fridays.
I am getting ready for the party.
Can you get me some water?
intermediateSentences: I finally got the promotion I've been working toward.
She's getting better at handling difficult clients.
We should get this sorted out before the deadline.
He got really frustrated when the system crashed.
I'm getting used to the new schedule.
Did you get a chance to review my email?
It took a while, but I finally got the hang of it.
They got stuck in traffic on the way to the airport.
I don't quite get what you're trying to say.
We've got a lot of work to do before the launch.
businessSentences: We got positive feedback from the client on the demo.
I'll get back to you by the end of the day.
Let's get this project moving as soon as possible.
She got promoted to team lead last month.
We need to get approval before proceeding.
I got the numbers you asked for.
The team is getting close to finishing the release.
Did you get a chance to speak with the vendor?
We've got a tight deadline for this deliverable.
Let's get everyone aligned before the call.
questions: What time do you usually get up?
Did you get my email?
How did you get into this field?
What did you get for your birthday?
Have you got any questions for me?
How do I get to the train station from here?
When will you get back from your trip?
Did you get the results of your test?
How long did it take you to get used to the job?
Can you get this done by Friday?

commonMistakes.why: 'Get home' doesn't need 'to' - 'home' acts like an adverb here. Say 'get home', 'get there', 'get here' without a preposition.
'Get' is irregular - its past tense is 'got', not 'getted'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't get what means this.
commonMistakes.right: I don't get what this means.
commonMistakes.why: Word order matters in embedded questions - keep the subject before the verb ('what this means'), not question order ('what means this').

grammarTips: 'Get' is extremely flexible in spoken English and can replace 'become' (get tired), 'receive' (get a letter), 'buy' (get some milk), 'arrive' (get home), or 'understand' (I get it) - context tells you which meaning applies.
'Get' + past participle forms a common informal passive: 'get married', 'get fired', 'get invited' - these work like 'be married/fired/invited' but feel more everyday.
In American English the past participle is usually 'gotten' (I have gotten better), while British English typically keeps 'got' (I have got better).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Did you get my text about tonight?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Yeah, I got it. What time should I get there?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Around eight should be fine.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Great, I'll get going soon then.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'I ___ your message this morning.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Got' is the correct irregular past tense of 'get'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence uses 'get' correctly?
options: I am getting to home now.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Get home' takes no preposition - 'home' functions like an adverb here.

miniQuiz.question: She has finally ___ (get) used to the new system.
miniQuiz.explanation: In American English, the past participle of 'get' is 'gotten' (British English often uses 'got').

tenseUsage.past: I got a new job last month.
tenseUsage.present: I get up at seven every day.
tenseUsage.future: I will get a new job soon.

definition: To create, produce, or cause something to exist; also used in many fixed expressions (make a decision, make sense).
commonUses: Talking about creating or producing something
Fixed expressions (make a decision, make an effort, make sense)
Talking about causing a result (make someone happy)
Talking about earning money (make a living)
beginnerSentences: They made a plan for the weekend.
intermediateSentences: We need to make a decision by tomorrow.
She's made a real effort to improve her English.
That doesn't make sense to me.
He made a good impression at the interview.
I'm trying to make the most of this opportunity.
They made significant progress on the project.
This news made everyone in the office excited.
I've made a few changes to the original plan.
Would you mind making a copy of this document?
We're making good time on this trip.
businessSentences: We need to make a decision on the vendor by Friday.
The company made a profit this quarter.
Let's make sure everyone is on the same page.
I'll make the necessary changes to the report.
This partnership will make a big difference for us.
We made a strong pitch to the investors.
Can you make time for a quick call today?
The new policy is making things easier for the team.
We're making progress, but there's still work to do.
I want to make sure the client is satisfied.
questions: What do you usually make for breakfast?
Have you made a decision yet?
What makes you happy at work?
Did the presentation make sense to you?
How much money do you make in this job?
What time does that make it in your time zone?
Who made this decision?
What would make this project better?
Can you make it to the meeting tomorrow?
What made you choose this career?

commonMistakes.why: 'Make' and 'do' are often confused - use 'make' for creating something or reaching an outcome (make a decision, make a mistake, make progress), and 'do' for general activities or tasks (do homework, do the dishes).

commonMistakes.wrong: This makes sense to me sense.
commonMistakes.why: 'Make sense' is a fixed phrase - don't repeat 'sense' or rearrange the words.
'Make' is irregular - its past tense and past participle are both 'made', not 'maked'.

grammarTips: 'Make' and 'do' are frequently mixed up - as a rule of thumb, 'make' is for producing or creating something (make a cake, make money, make a decision), while 'do' is for activities without a specific end product (do exercise, do the laundry, do a favor).
Many fixed expressions use 'make', not 'do': make a mistake, make progress, make an effort, make sense, make a difference - these are worth memorizing as whole phrases.
'Make' can be followed by an object plus a base-form verb to show causation: 'This makes me think', 'The news made her cry' - no 'to' is used here.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Have you made a decision about the new apartment?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Not yet, but I'm making progress. I should decide by this weekend.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: That makes sense - it's a big decision.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Exactly, I want to make sure I get it right.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: I need to do a decision.
I need to make a decision.
miniQuiz.answer: I need to make a decision.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Make a decision' is the correct fixed expression - 'do' is not used here.

miniQuiz.question: Which pair uses 'make' vs 'do' correctly?
options: make the dishes / do a cake
do the dishes / make a cake
make the dishes / make a cake
do the dishes / do a cake
miniQuiz.answer: do the dishes / make a cake
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Do' is for general tasks (do the dishes); 'make' is for creating something (make a cake).

miniQuiz.question: She has ___ (make) a lot of progress this year.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Made' is the correct past participle of 'make', used after 'has'.

tenseUsage.past: I made dinner last night.
tenseUsage.present: I make dinner every evening.
tenseUsage.future: I will make dinner tonight.

definition: To pick up, carry, or move something; to require (time or effort); used in many fixed expressions (take a break, take care).
commonUses: Talking about carrying or moving something
Describing how long something requires ('This will take an hour')
Fixed expressions (take a break, take a look, take care)
Talking about transportation (take a bus, take a taxi)
beginnerSentences: I take the bus to work.
She takes her dog for a walk.
We took a lot of photos.
He took my pen by mistake.
I am taking a break now.
They took a taxi to the airport.
Can you take this box for me?
intermediateSentences: It took me a while to understand the new system.
She's taking on more responsibility at work these days.
We need to take this issue seriously.
He took the news better than I expected.
I'm going to take some time off next month.
Have you taken a look at the latest report?
This project will take longer than we initially planned.
They took a chance on a new approach, and it paid off.
I've taken your feedback into account.
We should take advantage of this opportunity while we can.
businessSentences: This will take about two weeks to complete.
Let's take a step back and reassess the strategy.
I'll take care of scheduling the follow-up call.
She took the lead on the client presentation.
We need to take these risks into consideration.
The company is taking steps to reduce costs.
Can you take notes during today's meeting?
We've taken on three new clients this month.
Let's take a moment to review the key points.
I'll take responsibility for the delay.
questions: How long does it take to get to the office?
Did you take the day off yesterday?
Who's taking notes in this meeting?
How long will this project take?
Have you taken any breaks today?
What size do you take?
Can you take my shift tomorrow?
Did you take the bus or drive?
Who took responsibility for the mistake?
What would it take to convince you?

commonMistakes.why: 'Take' is irregular - the past tense is 'took', not 'taked'.
Use 'make', not 'take', to describe causing a feeling or result. 'Take' is for duration, carrying, or acquiring, not for causing emotions.
After 'have', use the past participle (V3) 'taken', not the past simple 'took'.

grammarTips: 'Take' is irregular across all three key forms: take, took, taken - practice them together so 'have took' doesn't slip out by mistake.
Use 'take' to talk about duration: 'It takes 20 minutes to get there' - this is a very common structure worth mastering early.
Many everyday expressions use 'take': take a break, take a seat, take a look, take care, take your time - learning these as whole chunks makes your speech sound much more natural.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: How long will this meeting take?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Probably about thirty minutes. Do you need to take off early?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Yeah, I have to take my car to the mechanic.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: No problem, we'll take care of it before then.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'She ___ the last train home.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Took' is the correct simple past tense of 'take'.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'taken', not 'took'.

miniQuiz.question: How long does it usually ___ (take) to finish this task?
miniQuiz.explanation: Base form 'take' is used after 'does' in a present simple question.

tenseUsage.past: I took the bus yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I take the bus every day.
tenseUsage.future: I will take the bus tomorrow.

definition: To move toward the speaker or a particular place; to arrive or approach.
commonUses: Fixed expressions (come up, come across, come along)
beginnerSentences: She comes to class every day.
He came to see me yesterday.
I am coming to the party.
They came from a small town.
I didn't come to school today.
intermediateSentences: A great opportunity has just come up at work.
She's coming along well in her new role.
I came across an interesting article this morning.
We came to an agreement after a long discussion.
He's finally coming to terms with the decision.
This idea came from a conversation we had last week.
The results are coming in slower than expected.
I'm confident things will come together in the end.
She came up with a clever solution to the problem.
Let's see how the negotiations come out.
businessSentences: The client is coming to the office next Tuesday.
A few issues have come up during testing.
We came to a mutual agreement on the pricing.
The results are coming in ahead of schedule.
This proposal came from the marketing team.
Let's see what ideas come out of the brainstorm.
The budget approval finally came through.
She came prepared with all the data we needed.
We're coming close to finishing the first phase.
New opportunities keep coming our way this quarter.
questions: Where do you come from?
What time will you come tomorrow?
Did anything interesting come up today?
How did you come up with this idea?
Are you coming to the meeting?
When did this problem first come to light?
How did the interview come along?
Have you come across this issue before?
What made you come to this decision?
Will the package come today?

commonMistakes.why: 'Come' is irregular - its past tense is 'came', not 'comed'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I come from India since five years.
commonMistakes.right: I've been living in India for five years.
commonMistakes.why: 'Come from' describes origin, not duration - it doesn't combine naturally with 'since' or 'for'. Use present perfect continuous ('have been living') to talk about duration.
'Here' and 'there' don't take 'to' before them - say 'come here', not 'come to here'.

grammarTips: 'Come' describes movement toward the speaker or a shared point of reference, while 'go' describes movement away from it - 'Come here' vs 'Go there' captures this difference clearly.
The past tense and past participle of 'come' look different from most verbs you'll learn: came (V2) but come (V3) - notice that V1 and V3 are spelled the same.
'Come' combines with many particles to form phrasal verbs with distinct meanings: come across (find by chance), come up (arise), come along (make progress), come out (be revealed).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Are you coming to dinner tonight?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I'd love to! What time should I come?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Around seven. Come whenever works for you, though.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Great, I'll come straight from work then.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'She ___ to visit us last summer.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Came' is the correct simple past tense of 'come'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I have come here for before.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'come' (which happens to look like the base form here).

miniQuiz.question: A great idea just ___ (come) up during the meeting.
miniQuiz.explanation: Simple past 'came' is correct here since the meeting already happened.

tenseUsage.past: She came to the party last week.
tenseUsage.present: She comes to the office every day.
tenseUsage.future: She will come to the party next week.

definition: To perceive with the eyes; to understand or recognize; to meet or visit someone.
commonUses: Meeting or visiting someone ('see a friend', 'see a doctor')
Understanding something ('I see what you mean')
beginnerSentences: I see a bird in the tree.
She sees her friends every weekend.
We saw a movie last night.
He saw his old teacher at the store.
Did you see the news today?
I am seeing the doctor tomorrow.
Can you see the board from there?
intermediateSentences: I see what you mean about the schedule now.
She's been seeing a specialist about her back pain.
We saw a significant improvement after the changes.
I can see why you're worried about this.
Have you seen the latest version of the report?
It's good to see the team working together so well.
I saw this coming a mile away.
We'll have to wait and see how things turn out.
I've never seen him this frustrated before.
Let's see if we can find a better solution.
businessSentences: I see your point, but let's consider the risks too.
We saw a strong response to the new campaign.
Have you seen the updated figures from finance?
I'll see what I can do to speed up the process.
We're seeing steady growth in this market segment.
Let's see how the pilot program performs first.
I can see this becoming a bigger issue if we wait.
She saw an opportunity and acted on it quickly.
We'd like to see more detail before approving this.
I'll see you at the client meeting on Thursday.
questions: Did you see the email I sent this morning?
When was the last time you saw him?
Do you see any problems with this plan?
Have you seen this movie before?
Can you see what I'm talking about?
What do you see happening in the next few years?
Did you see anyone at the office today?
How often do you see your family?
Do you see yourself staying in this role long-term?
Have you seen my keys anywhere?

commonMistakes.why: 'See' is irregular - its past tense is 'saw', not 'seed'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am seeing what you mean.
commonMistakes.why: When 'see' means 'understand', it's a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous form - say 'I see', not 'I am seeing', when you mean 'I understand'.
After 'have', use the past participle (V3) 'seen', not the simple past 'saw'.

grammarTips: 'See' has three different forms worth keeping straight: see (base), saw (past), seen (past participle, always paired with have/has/had).
When 'see' means 'understand' ('I see what you're saying'), it's a stative verb and should stay in the simple form, not the continuous - avoid 'I am seeing' in this meaning.

'See', 'watch', and 'look at' aren't interchangeable: 'see' is about perceiving without much effort, 'watch' implies attention over time (watch a movie), and 'look at' implies deliberately directing your eyes (look at this photo).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Did you see the game last night?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I did! I couldn't believe that last-minute goal.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: I know, I've never seen anything like it.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: We should watch the next one together.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'I ___ her at the conference last year.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Saw' is the correct simple past tense of 'see'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I am seeing what you mean.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'See' meaning 'understand' is a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous form.

miniQuiz.question: Have you ever ___ (see) a total solar eclipse?
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'seen'.

tenseUsage.past: I saw him yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I see him every day at work.
tenseUsage.future: I will see him tomorrow.

definition: To have information or understanding about something; to be familiar with a person, place, or fact.
commonUses: Talking about familiarity with people or places
Common in polite disagreement ('I know, but...')
beginnerSentences: Did you know about the meeting?
They knew each other in school.
I didn't know you were here.
intermediateSentences: I've known her since we were in college together.
She knows this project inside and out.
We didn't know what to expect from the new system.
As far as I know, the meeting is still on.
He knew something was wrong the moment he walked in.
I wish I had known about this earlier.
You never really know how someone will react.
I know for a fact that the numbers are accurate.
She's known for being extremely detail-oriented.
Let me know if anything changes.
businessSentences: As far as I know, the budget hasn't been approved yet.
She knows the client's requirements better than anyone.
Let me know if you need any additional information.
We knew the risks going into this project.
I don't know if the vendor can meet this deadline.
He's known in the industry for his innovative ideas.
Do you know who's leading this initiative?
We need to know the exact figures before proceeding.
I knew this would come up eventually.
Please let the team know about the schedule change.
questions: Do you know how to use this software?
Did you know about the change in plans?
How long have you known each other?
Do you know where the nearest station is?
Did you know he was leaving the company?
How did you know I was here?
Do you know anyone who could help with this?
What do you know about this topic?
Did you know that this policy has changed?
How well do you know this city?

commonMistakes.why: 'Know' is a stative verb describing a mental state, not an action - it's not normally used in the continuous (-ing) form.

commonMistakes.right: I have known him for years.
commonMistakes.why: 'Know' is irregular (know, knew, known), and duration up to now needs the present perfect: 'have known', not the simple past 'knowed'.
To talk about a skill, use 'know how to' + verb, not 'know to' + verb.

grammarTips: 'Know' is a classic stative verb - like 'want', 'believe', and 'love' - so avoid the continuous form: say 'I know', not 'I am knowing'.
For duration that continues into the present ('I've known her for ten years'), use the present perfect, not the simple past - the action of 'knowing' hasn't stopped.
'Know how to' + verb expresses a learned skill ('I know how to drive'), while 'know' + noun expresses information or familiarity ('I know the answer', 'I know him').

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Do you know if the store is open today?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I'm not sure, actually. Let me check.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Thanks, I know you're busy, so no rush.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: No problem, I'll let you know in a few minutes.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Know' is a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous (-ing) form.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past participle: 'I have ___ him for ten years.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Known' is the correct past participle of 'know', used after 'have'.

miniQuiz.question: She ___ (know) how to fix this problem right away.
miniQuiz.explanation: Simple past 'knew' fits a completed past action.

tenseUsage.past: I knew the answer during the test.
tenseUsage.present: I know the answer now.
tenseUsage.future: I will know the results by tomorrow.

definition: To use your mind to consider, reason about, or form an opinion about something.
beginnerSentences: I think this is a good idea.
She thinks about her family often.
He thought the movie was great.
Do you think it will rain?
I am thinking about my next vacation.
They thought the test was easy.
Can you think of a solution?
What do you think about this plan?
intermediateSentences: I've been thinking about changing careers.
She thinks carefully before making decisions.
We thought the project would take longer than it did.
I think we should reconsider our approach.
He's thinking of starting his own business.
I hadn't thought about it that way before.
They think highly of her work ethic.
Let's think this through before we commit.
I think there might be a better solution.
She's always thinking three steps ahead.
businessSentences: I think we should postpone the launch by a week.
The team thought the proposal needed more detail.
What do you think of the new marketing strategy?
We're thinking about expanding into new markets.
I think this approach could save us time and money.
Let's think about the long-term impact of this decision.
She thought outside the box to solve the problem.
I don't think the client will approve this budget.
We need to think strategically about our next steps.
I think it's worth revisiting the original plan.
questions: What do you think about this idea?
Have you thought about the consequences?
What are you thinking right now?
Do you think this will work?
What made you think of that solution?
Did you think the presentation went well?
What do you think is the best option?
Have you thought about a career change?
Do you think we have enough time?
What do you think she meant by that?

commonMistakes.why: 'Think' is irregular - its past tense is 'thought', not 'thinked'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am thinking that you are right.
commonMistakes.why: When 'think' means 'have an opinion', it's normally used in the simple form, not continuous.

commonMistakes.wrong: I think so that it is true.
commonMistakes.why: 'Think so' is a complete fixed phrase meaning 'I believe that's true' - don't add extra words after it.

grammarTips: 'Think' is irregular with a shared past form: think, thought, thought.
'Think' meaning 'believe' stays in the simple form ('I think it's a good idea'), but 'think' meaning 'actively reasoning' can use the continuous ('I'm thinking about it right now').
'Think of' and 'think about' are often interchangeable, but 'think of' can also mean 'remember' or 'come up with'.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: What do you think about the new restaurant downtown?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I haven't tried it yet, but I've heard good things. What do you think?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: I think we should give it a try this weekend.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Sounds good, I'll think about what time works for me.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'I ___ about it all night.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Thought' is the correct irregular past tense of 'think'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I am thinking that you are right.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Think' meaning 'believe' is normally used in the simple form, not continuous.

miniQuiz.question: I have ___ (think) about this for a long time.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'thought'.

tenseUsage.past: I thought about the problem all night.
tenseUsage.present: I think about my goals every morning.
tenseUsage.future: I will think about your offer and let you know.

definition: To direct your eyes in order to see something; to search for something; to appear or seem.
commonUses: Talking about directing your eyes toward something
Fixed expressions (look forward to, look after, look into)
beginnerSentences: We looked for the keys everywhere.
Did you look at the menu?
I am looking for my phone.
Can you look after my bag?
I didn't look at the clock.
intermediateSentences: I'm looking forward to the weekend.
She looked into the issue and found the cause.
We should look at this from a different angle.
He's been looking after his parents lately.
I looked everywhere but couldn't find it.
This plan looks promising so far.
They're looking to hire a new manager.
I never looked back after making that decision.
She looked relieved when the results came in.
Let's look into other options before deciding.
businessSentences: We're looking into a few new vendors this quarter.
The numbers are looking better than last month.
I'll look into this issue and get back to you.
We're looking forward to working with your team.
The client looked satisfied with the proposal.
Let's look at the data before making a decision.
We're looking to expand our team next quarter.
This deal looks promising for both sides.
I'll look over the contract before we sign.
The project is looking ahead of schedule.
questions: What are you looking for?
Did you look at my email?
How does the report look so far?
Are you looking forward to the trip?
Can you look into this problem for me?
What does the new office look like?
Did you look after the kids yesterday?
How do I look in this outfit?
Have you looked at the latest figures?
What are you looking to achieve this year?

commonMistakes.why: 'Look' needs 'for' when you're searching for something.
'Look' + adjective describes appearance directly; 'look like' + noun is used for comparisons - don't mix the two patterns.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am looking this book since morning.
commonMistakes.right: I have been looking for this book since morning.
commonMistakes.why: Duration with 'since' needs the present perfect continuous, not the plain continuous.

grammarTips: 'Look at' means directing your eyes toward something specific, while 'look for' means searching for something you can't currently see.
'Look' + adjective describes appearance ('You look great'), while 'look like' + noun compares appearance ('You look like your brother').
'Look' combines with many particles: look after (take care of), look into (investigate), look forward to (anticipate), look up (search for information).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Have you seen my glasses? I've been looking for them everywhere.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Did you look on the kitchen counter?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Yes, I looked there first. Still no luck.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Let's look in the car - you might have left them there.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: I am looking for my keys.
I am look for my keys.
miniQuiz.answer: I am looking for my keys.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Look for' means search - 'for' is required.

miniQuiz.question: Which is correct?
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Look' + adjective describes appearance directly, without 'like'.

miniQuiz.question: He has been ___ (look) after his sister all week.
miniQuiz.explanation: Present perfect continuous uses 'has been' + -ing form.

tenseUsage.past: I looked for my keys this morning.
tenseUsage.present: I look for my keys every morning.
tenseUsage.future: I will look for my keys before I leave.

definition: To desire or wish for something; to need or require something.
commonUses: Common in offers and invitations ('Do you want...?')
beginnerSentences: I want a cup of coffee.
We wanted to go home early.
He wanted to see the movie.
What do you want for dinner?
I don't want to be late.
She wanted to say thank you.
intermediateSentences: I've always wanted to visit Japan.
She wants to be taken seriously at work.
We wanted more time to prepare for the exam.
He wants nothing more than to succeed in this role.
I want you to know how much I appreciate this.
They wanted to make sure everyone was on board.
I don't want to rush this decision.
She's wanted this promotion for a long time.
We want to hear your honest feedback.
He wanted to apologize before it was too late.
businessSentences: The client wants a faster turnaround on this project.
We want to ensure quality isn't compromised for speed.
I want to discuss the budget before we proceed.
The team wanted more clarity on the requirements.
We want to build a long-term relationship with this client.
Management wants a status update by Friday.
I want to make sure everyone is aligned before we launch.
The company wants to expand into new markets.
We want your feedback on the new proposal.
I want this project to be a success for everyone involved.
questions: What do you want to do today?
Do you want anything from the store?
What did you want to talk about?
Do you want me to help you with this?
What do you want to achieve this year?
Did you want more information about the product?
What do you want for your birthday?
Do you want to join us for lunch?
What did the client want changed?
Do you want a break before we continue?

commonMistakes.why: 'Want' is a stative verb describing a desire, not an ongoing action - it's not normally used in the continuous form.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want that you help me.
commonMistakes.right: I want you to help me.
commonMistakes.why: 'Want' takes an object plus 'to' + verb, not a 'that' clause.
'Want' needs 'to' before the following verb - don't drop the 'to'.

grammarTips: 'Want' is a stative verb, so avoid the continuous form: say 'I want', not 'I am wanting'.
The structure is 'want' + object + 'to' + verb: 'I want you to call me', not 'I want that you call me'.
'Want to' is often shortened to 'wanna' in very casual speech, but keep the full 'want to' in writing and professional conversation.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: What do you want to do this weekend?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I want to relax at home, honestly. What about you?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: I wanted to go hiking, but I don't mind staying in either.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Let's decide tomorrow and see how we feel.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Want' is a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous form.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I want that you help me.
I want you to help me.
miniQuiz.answer: I want you to help me.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Want' takes object + 'to' + verb, not a 'that' clause.

miniQuiz.question: She ___ (want) to become a doctor when she grows up.
miniQuiz.explanation: Present simple 'wants' agrees with the third-person subject 'she'.

tenseUsage.past: I wanted a new laptop last year.
tenseUsage.present: I want a new laptop now.
tenseUsage.future: I will want a new laptop when this one breaks.

definition: To transfer something to someone; to provide or offer something.
commonUses: Talking about transferring or handing over something
Fixed expressions (give up, give in, give a chance)
beginnerSentences: I give her a gift every year.
Did you give her the message?
I am giving a presentation tomorrow.
They gave us a warm welcome.
Can you give me a hand?
I didn't give him my number.
intermediateSentences: I've given this a lot of thought.
She's always giving her best effort at work.
We gave the new approach a fair chance.
He gave up smoking last year.
I'm going to give this project my full attention.
They gave in to the client's demands eventually.
This has given me a new perspective on things.
She gave a detailed explanation of the process.
We should give it some time before judging the results.
He's given a lot back to the community over the years.
businessSentences: I'll give you an update by the end of the day.
The client gave positive feedback on the proposal.
We need to give this decision more thought.
She gave a great presentation at the conference.
Let's give the new process a chance before changing it again.
I'll give the team clear instructions before we start.
We were given a tight deadline for this project.
Can you give me your honest opinion on this?
The company gives employees flexible working hours.
I'll give you a call once I have more details.
questions: Can you give me some feedback on this?
Did you give her the report?
What did the client give as their reason?
Would you give me a second chance?
Who gave you this information?
Can you give a quick summary of the meeting?
Did you give up on the idea already?
What would you give to have more free time?
Can you give me an example?
How much time should we give this project?

commonMistakes.why: 'Give' is irregular - its past tense is 'gave', not 'gived'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have gave him the report.
commonMistakes.right: I have given him the report.
commonMistakes.why: After 'have', use the past participle (V3) 'given', not the simple past 'gave'.
When both objects are pronouns, use 'to' rather than putting them next to each other.

grammarTips: 'Give' is irregular with three distinct forms: give, gave, given.
'Give' can take two objects without a preposition ('give her a book') or one object with 'to' ('give a book to her') - pronoun combinations usually prefer 'to'.
Many fixed expressions use 'give': give up (quit), give in (surrender), give a chance, give it a try.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Can you give me a hand with these boxes?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Sure, just give me a second to finish this call.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: No rush, take your time.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Okay, I'll give you a shout when I'm free.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'She ___ me great advice.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Gave' is the correct simple past tense of 'give'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I have gave him the file.
I have given him the file.
I have give him the file.
I has given him the file.
miniQuiz.answer: I have given him the file.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'given'.

miniQuiz.question: Please ___ (give) me a call when you're free.
miniQuiz.explanation: Base form 'give' is used after 'please' in a polite request.

tenseUsage.past: I gave her a gift yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I give her a gift every year.
tenseUsage.future: I will give her a gift tomorrow.

definition: To employ something for a purpose; to make use of something.
commonUses: Talking about how tools or objects are employed
Talking about being accustomed to something ('be used to')
Common in instructions and technical contexts
beginnerSentences: I use my phone every day.
She uses a laptop for work.
We used a map to find the way.
He used my pen without asking.
Did you use the new app?
I am using this software now.
They used to live in this city.
This app is easy to use.
intermediateSentences: I've been using this method for years, and it works well.
She's used to working long hours.
We used all the resources available to us.
He's using a new strategy to improve sales.
I used to think differently about this topic.
They're using data to make better decisions.
This tool is widely used across the industry.
I'm not used to this kind of pressure.
We should use this opportunity wisely.
She used her experience to guide the team.
businessSentences: We use this software to track our sales pipeline.
The team used the feedback to improve the product.
I'll use this data to build the quarterly report.
We're using a new vendor for our supplies this year.
This approach has been used successfully by other companies.
Let's use this meeting to align on priorities.
The client used our services for over five years.
We should use this budget more efficiently.
I used the extra time to review the contract in detail.
The company is using AI to streamline operations.
questions: What software do you use for this task?
Did you use the discount code?
How often do you use this app?
What method did you use to solve this?
Are you used to working remotely?
Did you use to live abroad?
Can I use your phone for a minute?
What tools do you use in your daily work?
How is this data being used?
Did you use all the ingredients in the recipe?

commonMistakes.wrong: I used to using this tool.
commonMistakes.right: I'm used to using this tool.
commonMistakes.why: 'Used to' (a past habit) and 'be used to' (accustomed to) are different structures - the second needs 'be' before it.

commonMistakes.wrong: I use to go there every summer.
commonMistakes.right: I used to go there every summer.
commonMistakes.why: For past habits, the correct phrase is 'used to' (with a 'd'), not 'use to'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This tool is use by many people.
commonMistakes.right: This tool is used by many people.
commonMistakes.why: In the passive voice, the past participle 'used' is needed, not the base form.

grammarTips: 'Used to' + base verb describes a past habit that no longer happens: 'I used to smoke' means you don't anymore.
'Be used to' + -ing means being accustomed or familiar with something - don't confuse this with 'used to' for past habits.
In the passive voice ('is used', 'was used'), always use the past participle 'used', regardless of tense.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Do you use this app for tracking expenses?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Yeah, I've been using it for about a year now.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Is it easy to use?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Definitely, I got used to it within a few days.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: I use to wake up early.
I used to wake up early.
I am use to wake up early.
I uses to wake up early.
miniQuiz.answer: I used to wake up early.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Used to' (with a 'd') is the correct phrase for a past habit.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence describes being accustomed to something?
options: I am used to this weather.
I am using to this weather.
miniQuiz.answer: I am used to this weather.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Be used to' + noun/-ing shows being accustomed to something.

miniQuiz.question: This tool is widely ___ (use) across the company.
miniQuiz.explanation: Passive voice needs the past participle 'used'.

tenseUsage.past: I used this app last year.
tenseUsage.present: I use this app every day.
tenseUsage.future: I will use this app for my next project.

definition: To discover or locate something; to come to realize or perceive something.
commonUses: Talking about discovering or locating something
Expressing opinions after evaluation ('I find this interesting')
beginnerSentences: I find my keys every morning in the same place.
We found a great restaurant nearby.
He found his phone under the couch.
I am finding this book difficult.
intermediateSentences: I've found it hard to focus lately.
She found out about the change last week.
We found a way to solve the problem together.
He's finding the new role quite challenging.
I found myself agreeing with her point of view.
They found the training program very useful.
We should find time to catch up soon.
I found out that the meeting was rescheduled.
She found a creative solution to the budget issue.
It took a while, but we finally found common ground.
businessSentences: We found a more cost-effective supplier.
I found some inconsistencies in the report.
The team found a workaround for the technical issue.
We need to find a way to reduce costs this quarter.
I found the client's feedback very helpful.
Let's find a time that works for everyone.
We found the survey results quite surprising.
I'll find out and get back to you shortly.
The audit found several areas for improvement.
We found a strong candidate for the position.
questions: Did you find what you were looking for?
How did you find out about this job?
Did you find the meeting useful?
Can you find time for a quick call today?
What did you find most challenging about this?
Where did you find this information?
Did you find a solution to the problem?
How do you find working from home?
Have you found a replacement yet?
What did the report find?

commonMistakes.why: 'Find' is irregular - its past tense is 'found', not 'finded'.
'Find' + object + adjective is the correct word order to express an opinion.
After 'have', use the past participle (V3) 'found', not the base form 'find'.

grammarTips: 'Find' is irregular with a shared past form: find, found, found.
'Find' + object + adjective expresses an opinion after experience: 'I find this course useful'.
'Find out' means to discover information, often through investigation, while plain 'find' usually means locate something.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Did you find the restaurant we talked about?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Yes, I found it easily using the map app.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Great, how did you find the reviews?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I found them mostly positive, so I made a reservation.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'I ___ my wallet under the bed.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Found' is the correct irregular past tense of 'find'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Find' + object + adjective is the correct order for expressing an opinion.

miniQuiz.question: We have ___ (find) a great solution to this problem.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'found'.

tenseUsage.past: I found my keys this morning.
tenseUsage.present: I find my keys every morning in the same spot.
tenseUsage.future: I will find a solution to this problem soon.

definition: To communicate information to someone, often in the form of facts, a story, or an instruction.
commonUses: Giving instructions or commands ('tell someone to do something')
Fixed expressions (tell the truth, tell a story, tell apart)
beginnerSentences: Did you tell her about the party?
I am telling you the truth.
They told us to wait outside.
Can you tell me the time?
This tells me a lot about her character.
I didn't tell anyone about this.
intermediateSentences: I've been meaning to tell you something important.
She told me she was considering a new job.
We were told to expect some delays.
He's always telling jokes at meetings.
I can tell you're upset about something.
They told us the project was on track.
I told myself I would finish this by Friday.
She has a way of telling things straight.
We should tell the team about the changes soon.
I could tell something was wrong from his tone.
businessSentences: Please tell the client we'll follow up by Friday.
I was told the budget had been approved.
Can you tell me more about the requirements?
She told the team about the new policy.
We need to tell stakeholders about the delay.
I'll tell you as soon as I hear back.
He told us the numbers looked promising.
Let's tell the client honestly about the challenges.
I can tell this project means a lot to you.
We were told to prepare for a smaller budget this year.
questions: Can you tell me more about this?
Did you tell him about the meeting?
What did she tell you?
Can you tell the difference between these two?
Who told you about this opportunity?
Did anyone tell you the news?
Can you tell me how to get there?
What did the doctor tell you?
Did you tell your manager about the issue?
How can you tell if something is wrong?

commonMistakes.why: 'Tell' is irregular - its past tense is 'told', not 'telled'.
'Tell' takes a direct object without 'to' - say 'tell me', not 'tell to me'.
'Say' doesn't take a personal object directly - use 'tell' when naming who received the information.

grammarTips: 'Tell' is irregular with a shared past form: tell, told, told.
'Tell' and 'say' are often confused: 'tell' takes a person as an object ('tell me'), while 'say' needs 'to' before a person ('say to me').
'Tell' + person + 'to' + verb gives an instruction: 'She told me to wait'.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Did anyone tell you about the schedule change?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: No, nobody told me. What changed?

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: They told us the meeting moved to 3 PM.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Thanks for telling me - I would have missed it otherwise.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'She ___ me the good news yesterday.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Told' is the correct irregular past tense of 'tell'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Tell' takes a direct personal object without 'to'.

miniQuiz.question: He has already ___ (tell) everyone about the plan.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'has', use the past participle 'told'.

tenseUsage.past: I told her the news yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I tell her the news every time something changes.
tenseUsage.future: I will tell her the news tomorrow.

definition: To put a question to someone; to request something.
beginnerSentences: She asks for help when needed.
We asked him about his day.
He asked for a glass of water.
I am asking for your opinion.
They asked us to join them.
This question asks about your experience.
intermediateSentences: I've been meaning to ask you about the project.
She asked a lot of thoughtful questions during the interview.
We asked around, but no one had any information.
He asked to be excused from the meeting.
I'm asking for your honest opinion here.
They asked if we could extend the deadline.
I should have asked for clarification earlier.
She's not afraid to ask for what she needs.
We were asked to submit our reports by Friday.
I asked myself if this was really the right choice.
businessSentences: The client asked for a revised proposal.
I'll ask the team for their input before we proceed.
She asked some tough questions during the review.
We were asked to cut costs by ten percent.
Can I ask what your budget range is?
He asked for an extension on the deadline.
I'm going to ask for feedback after the presentation.
They asked us to provide references.
Let's ask the client directly what they need.
I asked my manager for advice on this situation.
questions: Can I ask you a question?
Did you ask for permission?
What did she ask you about?
Did anyone ask about the schedule change?
Can you ask him to call me back?
Did you ask for a discount?
What would you ask if you could ask anything?
Did they ask you to stay longer?
Who should I ask about this issue?
Did you ask her out yet?

commonMistakes.why: 'Ask' takes a direct object without 'to' when asking someone something.

commonMistakes.wrong: He asked me that I help him.
commonMistakes.right: He asked me to help him.
commonMistakes.why: 'Ask' + person + 'to' + verb is the correct structure for requests, not a 'that' clause.
Don't forget the article 'a' before a singular countable noun like 'question'.

grammarTips: 'Ask' takes a direct personal object without 'to': 'ask him', 'ask her', not 'ask to him'.
'Ask' + person + 'to' + verb expresses a request: 'I asked him to help'.
'Ask for' means to request something specific: 'ask for help', 'ask for a refund'.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: Can I ask you something personal?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Sure, go ahead and ask.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: I wanted to ask how you stay so organized.

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Honestly, I just ask myself what's most important each day.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: I ask to him a question.
I ask at him a question.
I ask for him a question.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Ask' takes a direct personal object without 'to'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: He asked me that I help him.
He asked me to help him.
He asked that I helping him.
miniQuiz.answer: He asked me to help him.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Ask' + person + 'to' + verb is the correct structure for a request.

miniQuiz.question: She has already ___ (ask) for an extension.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'has', use the past participle 'asked'.

tenseUsage.past: I asked her a question yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I ask her a question every class.
tenseUsage.future: I will ask her a question tomorrow.

definition: To perform a job or task; to function properly; to make an effort toward a goal.
commonUses: Talking about effort toward a goal
Fixed expressions (work out, work on, work through)
beginnerSentences: I am working on a project.
They worked together on the report.
intermediateSentences: I've been working on this proposal for days.
She works closely with the marketing team.
We worked through several options before deciding.
He's working toward a promotion this year.
I'm not sure this strategy is working.
They worked overtime to meet the deadline.
This arrangement seems to be working well for everyone.
I've worked with difficult clients before.
We need to work out the details before moving forward.
She worked her way up to a leadership position.
businessSentences: We're working on a new product launch.
I worked closely with the design team on this.
This process isn't working as efficiently as it should.
We need to work out a better pricing strategy.
She's worked in this industry for over ten years.
Let's work together to find a solution.
The new system is working well so far.
I'll work on the presentation over the weekend.
We worked hard to meet the client's expectations.
The team is working under a tight deadline.
questions: Where do you work?
How long have you worked here?
Does this app work on your phone?
What are you working on right now?
Did the plan work out?
Do you work well under pressure?
How does this device work?
Did you work late yesterday?
What team do you work with?
How did the negotiation work out?

commonMistakes.why: Don't drop 'am/is/are' before '-ing' in continuous tenses, and don't use the -ing form alone as a main verb.
After 'doesn't', use the base form of the verb, not the -s form.

commonMistakes.wrong: I work since five years in this company.
commonMistakes.right: I have worked at this company for five years.
commonMistakes.why: Duration up to now needs the present perfect, and 'for' with a length of time, not 'since'.

grammarTips: 'Work' is a regular verb (work, worked, worked), unlike many of the most common English verbs.
Use present simple 'work' for permanent facts and present continuous 'am working' for temporary or current actions.
Many useful expressions use 'work': work out (exercise, or resolve successfully), work on (make progress on something), work through (deal with something step by step).

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: How's the new job working out?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Really well, actually. I'm working with a great team.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: That's great to hear. What are you working on these days?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: Mostly a big product launch we've been working toward for months.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'doesn't', use the base form of the verb: 'work', not 'works'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence correctly expresses duration up to now?
options: I work here since five years.
I have worked here for five years.
I am working here since five years.
I worked here since five years.
miniQuiz.answer: I have worked here for five years.
miniQuiz.explanation: Duration up to now needs present perfect ('have worked') with 'for', not 'since'.

miniQuiz.question: She is currently ___ (work) on a new project.
miniQuiz.explanation: Present continuous uses 'is' + -ing form for a current, temporary action.

tenseUsage.past: I worked late yesterday.
tenseUsage.present: I work in an office every day.
tenseUsage.future: I will work from home tomorrow.

definition: To experience an emotion or physical sensation; to have an opinion or impression about something.
commonUses: Common in polite check-ins ('How are you feeling?')
beginnerSentences: We felt nervous before the exam.
I am feeling much better now.
They felt relieved after the news.
Can you feel the cold wind?
I didn't feel well this morning.
intermediateSentences: I've been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately.
She feels strongly about this issue.
We felt confident going into the meeting.
He's feeling more comfortable in his new role.
I feel like we should reconsider this decision.
They felt let down by the lack of communication.
I'm starting to feel more optimistic about this project.
She felt a sense of accomplishment after finishing the report.
We should check in and see how the team is feeling.
He felt it was important to address the issue directly.
businessSentences: I feel we should revisit this proposal.
The team felt confident about hitting the target.
How do you feel about the new strategy?
We felt it necessary to escalate this issue.
The client feels the pricing is too high.
I feel strongly that we should invest in this area.
The team is feeling the pressure of the tight deadline.
We felt reassured after the client's positive feedback.
I feel this decision will benefit the company long-term.
How does the team feel about the upcoming changes?
questions: How do you feel about this decision?
Did you feel prepared for the interview?
How are you feeling today?
Do you feel confident about the presentation?
How did you feel when you heard the news?
Does this fabric feel comfortable to you?
How does the team feel about the changes?
Did you feel nervous before the exam?
How do you feel about working overtime?
Did you feel supported by your manager?

commonMistakes.why: 'Feel' is irregular - its past tense is 'felt', not 'feeled'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I feel that I am tired.
commonMistakes.why: When describing your own physical or emotional state, use 'feel' + adjective directly.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am feeling agree with you.
commonMistakes.why: 'Feel' meaning 'have an opinion' takes a 'that' clause or adjective, not 'agree' directly after it.

grammarTips: 'Feel' is irregular with a shared past form: feel, felt, felt.
'Feel' + adjective describes your own state directly ('I feel tired'), while 'feel that' + clause expresses an opinion.
'Feel like' + noun/-ing expresses a desire or preference: 'I feel like pizza tonight'.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: How are you feeling after the trip?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: A bit tired, but overall I feel great.

dailyConversation.speaker: A
dailyConversation.line: That's good to hear. Do you feel ready for tomorrow's meeting?

dailyConversation.speaker: B
dailyConversation.line: I think so. I feel pretty well prepared.

miniQuiz.question: Choose the correct past tense: 'She ___ nervous before the interview.'
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Felt' is the correct irregular past tense of 'feel'.

miniQuiz.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: I feel that I am tired.
miniQuiz.explanation: 'Feel' + adjective directly describes your state.

miniQuiz.question: I have ___ (feel) much better since I started exercising.
miniQuiz.explanation: After 'have', use the past participle 'felt'.

tenseUsage.past: I felt nervous before the exam.
tenseUsage.present: I feel nervous before every exam.
tenseUsage.future: I will feel nervous before the exam tomorrow.
